title: New York's property-tax cap and Riverhead's overrides

capBasics.law: Chapter 97 of the Laws of 2011, codified for towns at General Municipal Law §3-c; the State Comptroller (OSC) publishes the formula and collects a tax-cap compliance filing from every local government each year.
capBasics.limit: It caps the tax LEVY (the total dollars raised from property taxes, not the rate). OSC's formula is: prior-year levy × a tax-base-growth factor, plus prior-year PILOTs, all multiplied by an 'allowable levy growth factor' (the lesser of 2% or inflation — it has been the full 2% in recent years), minus coming-year PILOTs, plus any unused carryover — then certain costs are EXCLUDED (notably pension-contribution growth above two percentage points and some tort judgments and capital). The headline is '2%,' but the real ceiling is usually a bit higher once growth and exclusions are added.
capBasics.override: A town may exceed the limit only if it BOTH (1) passes a local law authorizing the override by a 60% vote of the governing body and (2) does so before adopting the budget. On a five-member board, 60% is three votes — so the vote is usually easy to reach; the step towns miss is adopting the local law.

finding.headline: Riverhead exceeded the cap five years running (2018–2022) without ever adopting the required override local law.
finding.cause: The town's independent auditor traced it to an error in calculating the tax cap for the 2018 fiscal year, which then carried forward.
finding.auditQuote: The Town exceeded the 2% property tax cap for the year ended December 31, 2022 without adopting a local law in accordance with the New York State enacted Chapter 97, Laws of 2011 Real Property Tax Levy Cap and Mandate Relief Provisions.
finding.correction: The Town adopted a local law and exceeded the 2% cap, inclusive of allowable exclusions, in 2023.

implications.title: It's a compliance violation, not a rounding issue
implications.text: The tax-cap statute contemplates that a levy raised above the limit without proper authorization be placed in reserve and used to reduce the following year's levy. Five years over the cap with no override law is a genuine internal-controls failure, not a technicality.

implications.title: It points to weak controls over the levy calculation
implications.text: A miscalculation that went uncorrected from 2018 through 2022 means the number that sets residents' taxes wasn't being independently checked against the state formula each year.

implications.title: Nine straight years over the cap (2018–2026)
implications.text: Put the two halves together and Riverhead has exceeded the state tax cap every single year from 2018 through 2026 — the first five (2018–2022) without the required override law, the last four (2023, 2024, 2025, 2026) with it. Piercing the cap is not an occasional emergency here; it is simply how the Town budgets.

implications.title: The Town now overrides the right way — but overriding is the new normal
implications.text: Since the 2018–2022 lapse, the Board has done it correctly every time: 2023, 2024 (4.86% levy), 2025 (7.89% levy — the largest hike since the cap began in 2012), and 2026 (7.74% levy) were all proper, public overrides. The 2025 and 2026 budgets were Supervisor Tim Hubbard's (R); for 2026 his board unanimously adopted the override local law first, then passed the $121.1M budget (rate $67.031 to $71.549 per $1,000) on November 18, 2025 — just days before Hubbard narrowly lost re-election to Jerry Halpin (D) by 37 votes, so it was Halpin who took office in January 2026 to administer a budget he did not write. The worry is no longer the paperwork — it's that a 7–8% levy increase, far above the ~2% cap, has become the Town's routine, which puts real pressure on 2027 and beyond.

levyContext.note: General Fund tax levy by year (context for the growth pressure, not the town-wide cap measure). The cap applies to the total levy across all town funds; this series is the General Fund portion the site tracks.

capStatus.label: Over cap — override local law adopted (corrected)
Over cap — override local law adopted (unanimous); 4.86% levy / 4.54% rate increase
Over cap — override local law adopted (unanimous, Nov 19, 2024); 7.89% levy — the largest hike since the cap began in 2012
Over cap — override local law adopted Nov 18, 2025 (unanimous); 7.74% levy / 6.74% rate increase

sources: Town of Riverhead 2022 Audited Basic Financial Statements, Management's Discussion & Analysis (tax-cap note).
Town of Riverhead 2021 Independent Auditor's Report (origin of the 2018 tax-cap calculation error).
RiverheadLOCAL, “Riverhead’s 2026 tentative budget calls for town-wide tax rate increase of 6.74%” (Sept. 29, 2025).
RiverheadLOCAL, “Riverhead Town Board approves 2026 budget” (Nov. 20, 2025) — the Board unanimously adopted the override local law before passing the $121.1M budget.

RiverheadLOCAL, “Aguiar’s proposed 2024 budget: … 4.9% tax levy hike, piercing cap” (Oct. 2, 2023) and “Town’s 2024 operating budget is adopted” (Nov. 16, 2023) — the 2024 budget pierced the cap with an override local law (4.86% adopted levy; $69.4M town-wide).

East End Beacon, “With recount complete, Riverhead Town Supervisor Tim Hubbard concedes” (Nov. 25, 2025) — Halpin defeated Hubbard 3,958–3,921 (37 votes), confirmed on manual recount; the 2026 budget had already been adopted under Hubbard on Nov. 18, 2025.

Riverhead News-Review, “Year in Review: Riverhead Town pierces state-mandated tax cap” (Dec. 2024) — the 2025 budget overrode the cap by local law at a 7.89% levy hike, the town's largest since the cap took effect in 2012.

NYS Office of the State Comptroller — Real Property Tax Cap for Local Governments and Tax Cap Compliance (osc.ny.gov): the levy-limit formula and the annual compliance filing every local government must submit.
